symbols: red dress — humanity, the blood of the Passion, and Mary's share in her Son's suffering
blue mantle — heavenly grace, Mary's role as Queen of Heaven
standing Child — the active, sovereign Christ who comes not as passive infant but as Lord
blessing gesture — Christ's priestly and kingly office exercised even in infancy
coral necklace — traditional apotropaic symbol and allusion to Christ's future blood-shedding
classical columns — the new temple of the Incarnation, Christ as the pillar of the Church
distant landscape — the created world over which the Incarnate Word holds dominion
description: The Virgin Mary, depicted as a young woman with long golden-brown hair, sits behind a stone parapet upon which the Christ Child stands. She wears a red dress with a jeweled brooch at the neckline and a deep blue mantle. Her right hand steadies the Child, who stands upright, nude save for a thin white cloth, wearing a coral necklace and raising his right hand. The background features classical architectural columns framing views of a distant landscape with trees and sky on the left, and an ornate candelabrum on the right.
theological_description: The Madonna and Child is the central devotional image of the Incarnation: God made flesh, born of a woman (Gal 4:4). Perugino presents the theological paradox with quiet grace -- the infinite Creator of the universe is held by a human mother, dependent on her care yet simultaneously the one who sustains all things (Col 1:17). Mary's gentle supporting hand embodies the Theotokos doctrine affirmed at Ephesus (431): she is truly the God-bearer, not merely the bearer of Christ's human nature. The Child's standing posture and blessing gesture communicate His active lordship; even as an infant He exercises the priestly office, prefiguring the blessing He will extend from the cross. The coral necklace, a common motif in Italian Renaissance Madonnas, was traditionally understood as a protective amulet, but its blood-red color also foreshadows the Passion -- the precious blood that would be shed for the redemption of the world (1 Pet 1:18-19). The classical architecture frames Mary as the new Ark of the Covenant (Luke 1:35, cf. Exod 40:34-35), the living temple in which God chose to dwell among His people. The serene landscape beyond speaks of the restored creation that the Incarnation initiates.

scripture_refs.reason: The birth of Jesus, the foundational event that the Madonna and Child image celebrates.
When the fullness of time had come, God sent forth His Son, born of a woman -- the theology of the Incarnation.
The virgin shall conceive and bear a son, Immanuel -- the prophetic basis for the image.
He is the image of the invisible God, in Him all things hold together -- the infant who sustains creation.
The Holy Spirit will come upon you and the power of the Most High will overshadow you -- Mary as the new Ark overshadowed by divine presence.
Redeemed with the precious blood of Christ -- the coral necklace as foreshadowing of the Passion.
The glory of the Lord filled the tabernacle -- Mary as the living tabernacle of the divine presence.
